richContent.whyTitle: Codec, Bitrate, and Resolution — What HD Really Means for Reels
richContent.whyBody: When people say a Reel is 'HD,' they usually mean it looks sharp on screen — but technically, HD is a bundle of three things working together. Resolution is the pixel grid (1080p means roughly two million pixels per frame). The codec (H.264 for most Instagram Reels) is the compression method that shrinks file size while keeping detail. Bitrate is how much data each second of video carries — higher bitrate usually means less blocky motion and clearer text. FastVidl preserves the highest bitrate copy Instagram's CDN still hosts, so you are not getting a re-compressed stream meant for adaptive mobile playback.

richContent.whyTitle: What 1080p Actually Means — and Why Pixel Count Matters on Modern Screens
richContent.whyBody: 1080p, or Full HD, means the video frame is 1920 pixels wide by 1080 pixels tall in landscape orientation — for vertical Reels, those dimensions flip to 1080 wide by 1920 tall. That is over two million pixels per frame, enough to look crisp on today's 6-inch phone displays and even on many tablets when held at arm's length. Lower tiers like 720p (1280x720) or 480p cut pixel count dramatically, which shows up as soft edges, visible compression blocks, and unreadable small text. FastVidl targets the true 1080p file Instagram stores when available, without upscaling a smaller source to fake Full HD.
